data.short_description: Kishōtenketsu beat sheet for the Novel Studio app: the four-act East Asian structure that turns on a twist rather than a conflict, as an adoptable template. The acts live in this entity's lore; a novel adopts the sheet in its Blueprint, and its scenes' beat property uses these act names exactly.

lore.title: Beats
lore.content:
The four acts of kishōtenketsu (起承転結), the East Asian structure built on a twist rather than a conflict, in story order. Acts are rough quarters — the third often runs short and the fourth shorter. Scenes' beat property must use these ASCII names exactly as spelled (Ki, Sho, Ten, Ketsu — the macron spellings shō/ten/ketsu appear only in prose).

THE SHAPE IS NOT CONFLICT-DRIVEN: no antagonist is required and nothing needs to be at war. The engine is recontextualization — the Ten changes what the first two acts meant, and the Ketsu lets the pieces settle into a new whole. When reviewing a novel on this sheet, do not flag "no rising conflict" as a structural gap; ask instead whether the Ten genuinely reframes what came before.

Ki (act 1, introduction) — establish: the characters, the place, the texture of ordinary life. This act earns the reader's attention through interest and specificity, not threat; what is planted here is what the Ten will turn.

Sho (act 2, development) — deepen: expand on the Ki's elements without introducing a major turn. Daily life continues, relationships thicken, details accumulate — seemingly simple, and quietly loading everything the twist will need.

Ten (act 3, twist) — turn: an unexpected element — a new perspective, an interruption, a revelation — recasts acts 1 and 2. It need not be a conflict or a betrayal; a stranger's arrival or a change of vantage will serve, so long as the story's earlier material now reads differently.

Ketsu (act 4, conclusion) — settle: bring the original elements and the twist together into a new stable meaning. The ending's job is harmony or resonance, not victory — the reader should feel the whole reorganize, not a foe defeated.
